content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 270 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_002_page_001.jpg

### Warning Against False Teachers

1. But there were also false prophets among the people of Israel, just as there will be false teachers among you. They will cleverly introduce destructive heresies, even going so far as to deny the Master who bought their freedom. In doing so, they bring swift destruction on themselves.

2. Many people will follow their shameful ways, and because of them the way of truth will be slandered.

3. In their greed they will make up clever lies to exploit you. But the judgment pronounced against them long ago has not been idle, and their destruction has not been sleeping.

### God Knows How to Rescue and Punish

4. For God did not spare the angels who sinned. He threw them into the dark pits of the underworld, where they are being held for judgment.

5. And God did not spare the ancient world either. He brought the flood on a world of ungodly people, but he protected Noah, a preacher of righteousness, along with seven others.

6. God also reduced the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, condemning them to total destruction. He made them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly.

7. But God rescued Lot, a righteous man who was distressed by the shameless immorality of the wicked people around him.

8. Yes, that righteous man, living among them day after day, was tormented in his soul by the lawless deeds he saw and heard.

9. So you see, the Lord knows how to rescue godly people from their trials, and he knows how to hold the unrighteous for punishment on the day of judgment --

10. especially those who follow the corrupt desires of their sinful nature and despise authority. These false teachers are bold and arrogant, and they show no respect for the glorious ones.

11. Even angels, who are far greater in power and strength, do not bring slanderous accusations against them before the Lord.

### The Character of False Teachers

12. But these people are like irrational animals -- creatures of instinct, born to be caught and destroyed. They slander what they do not understand, and like animals they too will be destroyed.

13. They will be paid back for the harm they have done. Their idea of a good time is to indulge in evil pleasures in broad daylight. They are blots and blemishes, reveling in their deceptions while they feast with you.

14. Their eyes are full of adultery and they never stop sinning. They lure unstable people into sin, and their hearts are trained in greed. What a cursed brood!

15. They have wandered off the right road and followed the path of Balaam son of Bosor, who loved the wages of doing wrong.

16. But a donkey -- a speechless animal -- rebuked him for his madness, speaking with a human voice and restraining the prophet's insanity.

17. These people are dried-up springs and mist driven by a storm. The deepest darkness has been reserved for them.

### Empty Promises, Enslavement

18. They make pompous speeches that are empty and meaningless. By appealing to the lustful desires of the flesh, they lure back people who have barely escaped from those who live in error.

19. They promise them freedom, but they themselves are slaves of corruption -- for you are a slave to whatever controls you.

20. If people have escaped the corruption of the world through knowing our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, and then get tangled up in these things again and are overcome by them, they are worse off at the end than they were at the beginning.

21. It would have been better for them never to have known the way of righteousness than to have known it and then turned away from the holy commandment that was passed on to them.

22. What the true proverb says has happened to them: "A dog returns to its own vomit," and, "A washed sow goes back to wallowing in the mud."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 -- "The Master who bought them":** The Greek despoten ton agorasanta autous carries a powerful metaphor from the slave market. The "Master" (despotes, not kurios) is the one who paid the purchase price for their freedom. These false teachers are denying the very one who redeemed them. Peter is not making an abstract theological point -- he is saying these people are biting the hand that freed them.

**Verses 4-9 -- Three examples, one lesson:** Peter builds a case with three historical precedents: fallen angels, the flood generation, and Sodom/Gomorrah. But the pattern is not just destruction. In each case, God also rescues the righteous -- Noah is saved through the flood, Lot is pulled out of Sodom. The lesson is double-edged: God will certainly judge the wicked, and God will certainly protect those who belong to him. Verse 9 is the thesis statement for the entire section.

**Verse 4 -- Sinaiticus reading "seirais" (chains/pits):** Codex Sinaiticus reads seirais (ropes/chains) rather than the later variant sirois (pits). Some manuscripts have seirois (pits of darkness). The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes binding and confinement. The reference is to the tradition of fallen angels (cf. 1 Enoch, Jude 6) -- angelic beings imprisoned in Tartarus (tartarosas), the only use of this word in the entire New Testament.

**Verse 6 -- Sodom "as an example":** The word hupodeigma (example/pattern) is significant. Peter is not just recounting history -- he is saying the destruction of Sodom is a preview of what awaits the ungodly. It is a visible, historical demonstration of divine judgment.

**Verses 10-12 -- Slandering the "glorious ones":** The doxas (glorious ones) are likely angelic beings. The false teachers apparently claimed authority over or spoke contemptuously about spiritual powers. Peter's point: even the angels, who vastly outrank these teachers, do not presume to pronounce judgment in such matters. These teachers rush in where angels refuse to tread.

**Verse 15 -- "Balaam son of Bosor":** Sinaiticus reads Bosor rather than the more common Beor. This is a well-known Sinaiticus variant. Balaam is the archetypal prophet-for-hire (Numbers 22-24) -- a man with genuine prophetic gifts who sold them to the highest bidder. The parallel is devastating: these false teachers are spiritual mercenaries, monetizing their access to the community of faith.

**Verse 16 -- The talking donkey:** The most absurd detail in the passage is also the sharpest. A donkey saw what a prophet could not. An animal without speech corrected a man who spoke for God. Peter uses this story to maximum rhetorical effect -- if a donkey can see through Balaam's greed, how much more should the church see through these teachers?

**Verses 18-19 -- The cruel irony:** They promise freedom but deliver slavery. This is the signature move of every false teacher in every age. The Greek is pointed: eleutheriam autois epaggellomenoi, autoi douloi huparchontes -- "promising them freedom, themselves being slaves." The sentence structure mirrors the hypocrisy: the promisers are themselves the enslaved. You are a slave to whatever defeats you.

**Verses 20-22 -- Worse than before:** Peter's warning is severe. Those who knew Christ and then returned to the old corruption are not back at square one -- they are worse off than before they believed. The proverbs (one from Proverbs 26:11, the other likely a common saying) are deliberately disgusting. Dogs and pigs were the most unclean animals in Jewish thought. Peter is not being cruel; he is being honest about the gravity of apostasy. The nature of the animal has not changed -- that is the terrible point.

**The chapter as a whole:** 2 Peter 2 is the most sustained polemic against false teachers in the New Testament. Peter's method is to surround the present danger with the weight of sacred history. The angels fell and were judged. The flood generation was judged. Sodom was judged. Balaam was judged. The pattern is inescapable. But woven through the judgment is rescue -- Noah, Lot, the faithful. The chapter is not primarily about fear; it is about discernment. Know what you are dealing with, and know that God knows how to deliver his people.

-- PNEUMA, AI Translator
